Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Profile the consortium partners, explain complementarity, and justify the partnership composition.
Write the Consortium as a whole section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.3 Consortium as a whole  
We, the AI-CUBE consortium, have 5 partners representing 3 EU countries (Italy, Spain, Germany). All partners in our consortium have well-defined tasks and responsibilities, and our technical and non-technical expertise corresponds well to the requirements of each of our WP tasks. The main advantage of our consortium is that we combine different expertise and past experiences that are fundamental to reach our goals and targets, as well as established and expected impact.  
Our project will benefit from the work of each partner in related Innovation European and National Roadmaps (SPIRE, EFFRA, ALICE, etc.), and this ensures our project will deliver a Roadmap that is compliant with the requirements derived also from the SPIRE Industry. We have been directly involved in several research & development projects, and among these, the following ones related to AI and BD: SELIS (H2020MG-6.3-2015; N.690588), CONNECTED FACTORIES (H2020 -FOF-11-2015, n. 723777), AGROinLOG (H2020-RUR-08-2016; N.727961), FUTURING (H2020 -NMBP-CSA-2016), DISRUPT (H2020 -FOF-11-2016, n.723541), MOVAID (H2020 -FOF10-2015, n.680754), and indirectly through network members in many others. Among these, project INSPIRE (SPIRE-06-2016; N.: 723748) has 3 partners of our consortium involved and was committed to finding new business models to strengthen the collaboration between process industry and discrete manufacturing along supply chain collaboration. The link with INSPIRE is very strong, and AI-CUBE can represent the follow-up of those activities.  
On top of that, we and our project team have participated in coordination actions, CSA, such as: MOBILITY4EU, SENSE, SETRIS, WINN, SoCool@EU, RECREATE, CRM_INNONET, BIO-TIC, BioLinX, NEW_InnoNet, EXPLORE, focused on identifying policies, establishing collaboration frameworks, identifying best practices, and development of roadmaps. Our team has strong expertise in supply chain management (ZLC), production processes (CNR), industrial symbiosis (PNO), data-driven technologies (IML and IRIS), and so on. This expertise will be complemented with the support of the Expert Group that will provide their expertise in other relevant projects.  
We have the ability to interface with the European Process Industry stakeholders. PNO is a key partner to the European Technology Platform (ETP) SusChem and the European Council for the Chemical Industry (CEFIC). Both PNO and IRIS have strong ties with A.SPIRE, the SPIRE association. CNR-STIIMA is a major partner of the European Technological Platform on Future Manufacturing Technologies (MANUFUTURE) initiative and founding members of EFFRA. And ZLC promoted the European Technology Platform in Logistics (ALICE) through the coordination of the EU-funded project WINN. IML are members of the Big Data Value Association (BDVA).  
We can directly exploit the national networks of each partner as a way to get directly in contact with the national research and innovation activities already running in the thematic areas of our project, as well as to significantly increase results diffusion.  
3.4 Resources to be committed  
The duration of our AI-CUBE project will be 24 months. The total budget is 597.806,88 € of which our Commission’s contribution will be 597.806,88 €. This consists of 444.745,50 € for personnel costs, 33.500 € for other direct costs, and 119.561,38 € to cover the indirect costs. Furthermore, a strong network shall be built that will be enabled to sustain over the project duration and beyond. Dedicated dissemination actions toward the European process industries and ICT community shall engage all stakeholders and raise awareness.  
All functions assigned in our project have gone through a thorough analysis of the prior experience and knowledge of each partner.  
The budgeted costs are predominantly personnel costs, and minor quantities have been assigned to cover costs for travel and accommodation and costs in relation to dissemination, communication, and exploitation activities. Additionally, a budget to cover travel expenses for members of the Expert Group has been allocated to our Coordinator’s budget. Within our Coordinator’s quota, a budget for the AI-CUBE project website (€ 10,000), the creation of at least one video and infographic materials (€ 3,000) for dissemination purposes, and the budget for the Business Model Game workshop (€ 10,000) has also been allocated.

Write the Consortium as a whole section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

AIDPATH     3 
1 Excellence 
Personalized cell & gene therapies are transformative novel treatments, but at the same time introduce unprecedented challenges in manufacture and clinical use. The most clinically advanced embodiment of these advanced therapies are CAR-T cells. In this cell therapy, immune cells (T lymphocytes) are engineered to express a synthetic chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) and are thereby redirected to seek and destroy cancerous cells in our body. CAR-T cell therapy is indeed a transformative new treatment in haematology with clinical proof-of-concept in patients with acute leukaemia, lymphoma (lymph node cancer) and multiple myeloma (bone marrow cancer)2. A conceptual appeal of this treatment is that our own immune cells are redirected – providing a personalized treatment - and that a single infusion of CAR-T cells can be sufficient to eliminate the cancerous cells and to establish immunological memory that protects us from tumour relapse (‘living drug paradigm’). CAR-T cell therapy in acute leukaemia and lymphoma is now an approved treatment that is highly demanded by patients and caregivers, and in the focus of attention of bio entrepreneurs, policy makers and regulators. Intriguingly, the success of CAR-T cell therapy as a role model for gene-engineered cellular immunotherapy in haematology and oncology has spurred the development of this treatment for novel applications in infectious diseases (targeting viral infections and fungal infections), chronic and autoimmune diseases.   
The challenge with engineered T-cell therapies is that they require a sophisticated process of manufacturing (i.e. sourcing, gene-engineering and expanding the cell product) and clinical use (i.e. preparing the patient, administering the cell product, monitoring the patient). Small alterations in either manufacturing and/or clinical use typically trigger a round of regulatory re-review and approval because they can change the safety and efficacy of the manufactured cells. This poses enormous challenges for us, the hospitals, that find ourselves in the epicentre of this CAR-T & cell therapy revolution: with our specialized health care infrastructure and highly trained health care providers we are the prime centres of choice for administration of the novel treatments. Accordingly, we are suddenly responsible for the complex tasks and logistics while still being 
                                                      
2 Nature. 2017 May 24; 545(7655): 423–431. doi:10.1038/nature22395. 
AIDPATH (Artificial Intelligence-driven, Decentralized Production for Advanced Therapies in the Hospital) is a high-energy EU consortium, dedicated to enable and to augment the next-generation of personalized medicine with gene-engineered immune cells at EU hospitals through the use of AI technology. The exemplary embodiment of gene-engineered immune cells in AIDPATH will be T cells modified to express a synthetic chimeric antigen receptor (CAR-T) that are already a revolutionary novel treatment in hematology and oncology, and will also be useful for treating infections and autoimmune diseases. Conventional CAR-T therapy is complicated by complex logistics and supply from centralized manufacturing facilities, and inflexible manufacturing and clinical use schemes that disregard patient and cell product characteristics, thereby limiting patient access and therapeutic outcome. 
We will apply top-notch AI technology to integrate patient-specific data and biomarkers in CAR-T therapy, and apply flexible manufacturing schemes to obtain CAR-T cell products with optimal fitness and anti-tumor potency. AI technology will also be applied in pre- and in-process controls to optimize scheduling and resource planning to reduce cost and hospital resource utilization and to augment patient access. We will establish a role model that integrates the hospital environment in a smart manner, incorporating also aspects of logistics, capacity planning, data management and cybersecurity by design. 
Our project is designed to enable the smart ‘bedside’ provision of personalized treatments directly at the ‘point-of-care’, and to accomplish end-to-end automation of hospital-based CAR-T manufacture. A key deliverable in AIDPATH will be an integrated software and hardware package that allows rapid dissemination of the ‘smart CAR-T manufacturing and clinical use’ concept to hospitals throughout EU member states.  
We bring together a world-class consortium, consisting of key opinion leaders in science and translational medicine, innovative biotech SMEs and commercial partners, patient advocates, policy makers and regulatory experts. Our consortium members are well-positioned to engage in a productive dialogue with regulators and patients to ensure acceptance and rapid implementation, adoption by EU hospitals and industry. A strategic aim in AIDPATH is to contribute to the sustainability and well-being of the EU health care systems, to place the EU at the forefront (ahead of the US and Asia) of the global CAR-T movement and to ensure that EU citizens will continue to have access to excellent health care.  
AIDPATH     4 
subject to enormous pressure to operate efficiently and economically. A ‘smart hospital of the future’ offering engineered T-cell therapy to our patients has to provide a sophisticated infrastructure, internal operations scheme and highly-trained dedicated staff that is proficient both in the manufacture and clinical use of these novel treatments. Only this way, clinical sites can ensure patient safety, positive clinical outcomes, and compliance with the socioeconomic demands of EU health care systems.  
A critical and vulnerable component for a ‘smart hospital of the future’ in cellular immunotherapy is the ability to carry out the GMP (good manufacturing practice) process. In conventional approaches, manufacturing is done manually with the assistance of some automation platforms. There is also the fact that variability from patient to patient means that the optimal manufacturing procedures can vary from batch to batch. Unlike centralized manufacturing, there is no economy of scale and there are multiple individualized manufacturing efforts happening in parallel3. The difficulties in decentralized engineered T-cell manufacturing have led to the consideration of ‘off-the-shelf’ allogeneic third party cell products and cell lines that can be administered to any patient. However, these allogeneic approaches carry a significant risk of graft versus host disease where the foreign immune cells attack non-malignant cells. Our immune system may also recognize the therapeutic cells as foreign which may lead to product rejection and limited therapeutic efficacy. Product rejection in the allogeneic setting may be mitigated by the use of immune system suppressing medications – which is however undesired as this would compromise our immunocompetence and increase the rate of infections.  
Artificial intelligence (AI), combined with process automation technology, has the potential to increase the efficiency and the quality of engineered cell therapy to a point that equals or excels what can be achieved with centralized manufacturing. AI approaches also have the potential to substantially increase the personalization of therapies through deep learning and the use of digital twinning concepts. This changes our role as hospitals: instead of just receiving and administering drugs, we assume an active role in the manufacturing of therapeutics. Smart hospitals of the future will evolve into decentralized but interconnected hubs that offer development, manufacture and administration of personalized advanced therapies such as CAR-T cells from a single source. AIDPATH introduces a trustworthy, AI powered approach that allows automated and intelligent production of the therapy in our hospital and bolstering the capacity of centralized manufacturing while ensuring manufacture is reproducible, safe and economically viable. Our project paves the way towards a future where engineered cell therapies are not limited by the challenges and inefficiencies of a complicated manufacturing process. 
Vision for the smart hospital of the future 
It is April 2027 and Stephanie, a young soccer player, develops a nose bleed during a match. The ball or any other player has not hit her. The coach and her parents rush to her side when they see that it is not letting up. Her parents have noticed that she has been more fatigued and complaining of feeling cold all the time. Later that night in the Emergency Room, her parents are startled when not just the nurse enters the room. A whole group enters, including a kindly doctor. The blood test shows that their daughter has leukaemia. A day later, once their confusion and fear have transformed into a firm resolve to overcome this leukaemia, they are back in the hospital clinic. 
Further evaluation shows that the young girl has acute lymphoblastic leukaemia. They listen intently as the doctor explains to them what her diagnosis means. The concerned look on the faces of her parents eases a bit when they hear that there is an effective therapy consisting of Stephanie’s own immune cells (T cells) engineered to express a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) that recognizes a surface molecule on the leukaemia cells and destroys them. A nurse draws more of her blood. That blood sample is packed in ice and taken down the hall to the AIDPATH system. The blood samples are attached to a machine which takes them up, and the technician enters in the patient's name which pulls up her medical record which the technician checks to make sure that all the relevant data fields are complete. Unbeknown to the patient, a complex AI analysis and logistical manufacturing chain is set into operation, just a couple of stories below her. The artificial intelligence that examined the patient and cell characteristics suggests a process sequence that is tailored precisely to the patient’s profile to provide the much desired CAR-T cell therapy. At the same time, all material and resources needed to produce the CAR-T cell batch are requested from central storage and allocated for by an intelligent software program. A trained person equips the machinery, which checks that everything is in place and correctly inserted. The process is triggered, T cells are extracted, and the process of modifying using a viral-vector free process starts. Then, the CAR-T cells are expanded in a bioreactor equipped with sensors that perform completely autonomously - even samples are taken and analysed automatically in a small robotic chamber. The results are fed back into an intelligent system control, which makes sure that the cells are manufactured fast and efficiently, and are provided in the highest quality. The only thing that our trained personnel have to do is to stand by, supervise the suggestions of the AI displayed through the system software and to monitor the process

Proposal Title: European Sustainable Bio-based nanoMAterials Community Proposal Acronym: BIOMAC DT-NMBP-04-2020 [BIOMAC] [2 of 68] 

1. Excellence 

1.0 Introduction and rational to BIOMAC 

Renewable resources are important driving alternatives of economic growth in many industrial sectors holding promises for sustainable development, unlike conventional petroleum-based ones. Bio-based products derived from biomass can pass through their entire life cycle alleviating obstacles associated with solid waste disposals, supporting at the same time the integration with a circular economy-based material loop. Yet, technical performance, economic feasibility and environmental sustainability should be further enhanced to meet new market requirements. [1] Especially the nano structured bio-based materials (NBM) are expected to become highly innovative materials with a great impact on European markets. Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC), cellulose nanocrystals (CNC), bacteria nanocellulose (BNC) and nanolignin (NL), which can be utilized as functional nano-additives of bio-based polymers, have progressively attracted considerable interest due to their abundance and potential application in multiple industrial sectors. The markets we address embrace a wide spectrum of industrial fields such as packaging, automotive, printed electronics (PE), construction, coatings and products for agriculture, covering strategic sectors of growing industry. A characteristic example is that of nanocellulose where the market is projected to register a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of more than 18% during the period of 2019-2024. [2] Further to this, the global lignin market size was valued at 793.7 million Euros in 2018 and is projected to expand at a CAGR of 1.9% from 2019 to 2025. [3] Constant technological development integrating lignin with nanotechnology is expected to drive the industry further, resulting in lignin nanomaterials being utilized as a foundation for the production of enhanced high added value end products. To sustain a viable plan, meeting the market’s demands, it is necessary for us to upscale the supply chain of biopolymers production. In order to further enhance the quality and performance of these polymeric matrices, the incorporation of nanoparticles (NPs) is essential to achieve advanced properties thanks to their functionalities. It is worth mentioning that the major challenge in the area lies at the fact that new product concepts are restricted to enter the market because end user applications remain at small lab scale tests having limited exploitation capacity for industrial deployment. To launch innovation within this growing sector, we propose the establishment of a self-sustainable ecosystem (Open Innovation Test Bed (OITB)) for upscaling of processes incorporating major developments in this area based on two pillars: to generate knowledge and to capture business value. Offering an open ecosystem, we focus on the creation and demonstration of novel manufacturing supply and value chains where technologies that have been developed up to TRL 4-5 will be upscaled and validated to TRL 7, accelerating the process towards market entrance. At the same time, we target to tackle obstacles that hinder the evolution of concept development in the field of nano-enabled bio-based materials and products, providing a unique opportunity of testing ideas in a well-structured open environment. Our ecosystem will act as a cluster of parallel actions that will be assembled in a structured manner deploying the classification of actions in four major groups of hubs. The core of our ecosystem consists of four Hubs (H). (1H) Pilot Plant Supreme Hub (PPSH) that includes three Pilot Lines Clusters (PLCs) covering the whole value chain, from biomass fractionation (Biomass Fractionation & Pre-treatment Cluster - BFPC) to intermediate chemicals and polymers (Intermediate Material and Nanocomposite Cluster – IMNC) and final nano-enabled biopolymers (Final Products & Formulation Cluster - FPFC). Subsequently, three more hubs surround the supreme hub, with the aim of assessing the feasibility and sustainability of the developed concepts: (2H) Validation Services Hub (VSH) where the services justify the feedstock, the technologies and the products (Quality Control Hub and Process Validation Hub), the (3H) Market Uptake Hub (MUH) that targets to support business perspective and legalization issues accompanied by the maintenance of the test cases, and finally the (4H) Value Chain assessment Hub (VCH) where the developed value chains will be assessed against economic and environmental criteria within the circular economy frameworks. The goal of BIOMAC is to provide open access PLs and services that will lower the cost of upscaling and accelerate the time-to-market for nano-enabled biomaterials, enhancing industrial sustainability and reducing waste following a circular model. To reach these targets, our ecosystem will function as a One-Stop-Shop (OSS) accessible at fair conditions and costs through the collaboration of our multidisciplinary consortium. This will be achieved through a Single Entry Point (SEP) which will be represented by the well-known and recognized European Association EUBP (European Bioplastics), handling all the business opportunities occurring at our ecosystem and leading the communication towards the clients. Additionally, EUBP has access to a significant network of decision makers in the world of bioplastics and a contact database for the association’s dissemination activities comprising over 8,000 contacts which will facilitate the awareness of BIOMAC. An internal mechanism to set workflows will be handled by us, the core partners, and a single offer will be defined to serve sustainably the clients’ requests.

1.1 Objectives 

A. Objectives related to the set-up and upgrade of the pilot line Hubs 

OA1: To set up an OITB consisting of three Clusters of PLs offering services in the field of nano-enabled bio-based materials. (PPSH – BFPC, IMNC & FPFC) 

OA2: To set up and build a transversal supporting service mechanism that will ensure a fast, reliable and cost-effective upscaling of bio-based material concepts. (VSH, MUH, VCH) 

OA3: To upgrade and appropriately modify the existing Pilot Lines through integration of monitoring and process control systems to cope with the biomass variability and to offer continuous and stable conversion of biomass towards the targeted bio-based monomers, biopolymers and nano-additives. (17 PL owners & VSH) 

OA4: To develop a holistic model of the proposed processes by demonstrating the link between characterization data and modelling/simulation approaches. (VSH, MUH) 

OA5: To validate the operation of the OITB through 5 selected TeCs (DIAD, NVMT, EVER, ACC & OHMAT – see Table 1.1.), and 5 more that will be introduced to the OITB through an open call. All TeCs will provide feedback and recommendations on the services provided by the OITB. 

B. Objectives related to the operation & sustainability of the ecosystem 

OB1: To establish a Single Entry Point (SEP) platform consistently with a One-Stop-Shop framework. Launch and operate an easily accessible platform in order to attract business cases entering the ecosystem as clients, during and post project. (MUH, VCH) 

OB2: To deliver key exploitable results (KER) and organize the IPR protection strategy (e.g. patents, industrial secrets, trademarks). Develop an IPR protection plan and a Data Management Plan (DMP) for the ecosystem. (VCH) 

OB3: To tap into niche markets, based on R&D expenditures and CAGR. Identify and penetrate industrial markets, by customer outreach (e.g. advertising, communication, participation in forums and fairs etc.). (All Partners) 

OB4: To continuously update the OITB business plan during the project. Overview of EU Research Policy, technology transfer/offer to stakeholders in both directions (technology push, and/or demand pull, stakeholder access). (VSH, MUH, VCH) 

OB5: To ensure the post-project sustainability of the ecosystem and create a sustainability plan attracting post project turnover of five times the EU investment within the first five years after the completion. (28 Core Partners) 

C. Objectives for the assessment of environmental, economic sustainability & circularity of the ecosystem 

OC1: To deliver production processes and products based on highest efficiency and lowest possible environmental impact and energy requirements throughout their life cycle by means of Social-LCA /LCA/LCC. (VSH, MUH, VCH) 

OC2: To develop a Decision Support Tool (DST) for the assessment of the value chain developed within the frames of the ecosystem prioritizing options and optimizing processes simultaneously against different criteria. (VCH) 

OC3: To validate the circularity of value chains of the ecosystem providing alternative solutions to shift towards circular business models, including End of Life (EoL). (VCH) 

OC4: To demonstrate the incorporation and update of nanosafety, regulatory, standardization, and training in the ecosystem, following EU (CEN, REACH) and international regulations and standards (ISO, ASTM). (MUH, VCH)

3.3 Consortium as a whole  
Our consortium consists of 13 complementary members including research institutes, SMEs, and a leading FMCG company. For effective management of our project and to listen to everyone’s voice, formation of a high-level board consisting of representatives from each member of our consortium is a must. Hence, a Project Board is appropriate for such a big consortium. As this is a high-level board and senior officials from each partner organization are involved, twice a year meeting is quite appropriate for this board. The creation of the role of a Project Coordinator, who will chair the project board, is very vital for the successful implementation of our project. The responsibility of this position is to coordinate between our consortium and the fund provider, EC. This position is important as we must submit several reports regularly to the EC. We will also have to inform important decisions taken in the project board meeting to the EC. For these regular and some critical jobs, such a position is very crucial. Two other key management positions, i.e., Project Technical Manager and Project Exploitation, Dissemination, and Communication Manager, are very crucial as their roles have been discussed previously. Finally, the WP leaders’ roles are vital as they will work at the grassroots level and they are responsible for delivering the actual deliverables of our project.  
Partner Role in the Project  
STEF SINTEF (Leading Norwegian RTO with extensive capabilities in soft matter processing and modelling & simulation) will be our project coordinator for the DIY4U project. STEF, along with CPI, will lead WP8 (Demonstration of Fablab and Integrated DIY4U Services), acting as MDF for one of the two fablabs to be developed. STEF will provide crucial end-user input (WP2) that will feed into platform and fablab design/development (WPs 3-6) and support DSC and CTM in the development of relevant soft matter modelling and process simulation capabilities for DIY4U.  
P&G  
P&G (Leading global manufacturer of FMCGs including detergents and soaps) will guide our project’s technical developments (WP3-WP8), ensuring that it remains industry-relevant and commercially viable. P&G will lead the manufacture and supply of base formulations (WP7) that will be customized in the MDFs (WP8). As a key exploitation partner, P&G will work with PIC and the other partners to develop the DIY4U business model and commercialization plan.  
CPI  
CPI (Part of the UK’s High Value Manufacturing Catapult, hosting the National Formulation Centre with capabilities in the processing of complex particles, complex liquids, and other material formulations, as well as capabilities in modelling and process engineering) will, similarly to STEF, lead WP8 (Demonstration of Fablab and Integrated DIY4U Services), acting as MDF for one of the two fablabs to be developed, and provide crucial end-user input (WP2) that will feed into platform and fablab design/development (WPs 3-6). CPI will additionally employ a combination of its formulation, process engineering, and modelling & simulation capabilities to lead the development of the LCA/LCCA model subplatform (WP4) and support DSC and CTM in the development of soft matter modelling & simulation capabilities and digital twin.  
VTT  
VTT (Leading European RTO with PAT, data analytics, and automation expertise and capabilities) will act as the leader of PAT activities in our project, developing integrated, miniaturized, and low-cost PAT methods and devices for the Fablabs based on near-infrared spectroscopy, camera-based measurements, and other techniques. VTT will also develop calibration methods and data analysis for the PAT devices.  
CTM  
Cetemet (robotic and automation expert, with process modelling expertise) will co-lead the extension of platform functionalities and testing, developing a digital twin and deploying soft matter modelling and simulation for using it to provide an open access collaborative production. Cetemet will enhance the simulation of soft matter by simulating it at the macroscopic level and correlate the results obtained at a microscopic level and analyze its interaction with the equipment that contains it. For this, it will carry out flow simulations with fluid particles to analyze their behavior and interaction with the structure through which the flow circulates by means of structural and fluid dynamics simulations with finite elements. The results will be implemented in the virtual twin in the platform and will contribute to the testing of the same. In addition, Cetemet will support the design and development of the fablab prototypes, using 3D CAD systems to obtain 3D models and drawings according to the requirements that will contribute to the development of the system. This activity will also involve the use of FE simulations for the assurance of the material resistance and the structural stability of the prototype.  
IRIS  
IRIS will function as the main software developer of our consortium, responsible for the programming and deployment of the KMC platform which will act as core controller and provide the backend and frontend for the whole DIY4U platform. IRIS will therefore also be responsible for the integration of the various advanced tools in the form of modules of the platform. IRIS will collaborate closely with EFF and DCC and the partners developing the extension functionalities/modules as well as fablab users (STEF and CPI) and the rest of our consortium.  
DCC  
Digital Catapult, as the blockchain expert of our consortium, will contribute to the understanding of user needs and requirements to then lead on the platform architecture design, having into account both the centralized and decentralized elements of the solution to enable efficient and trusted relationships between the different stakeholders involved in the fabrication of detergents. DCC will also have an advisory role during the development process of the platform, making sure the design proposed is being met. Moreover, DCC will support the dissemination and exploitation actions (especially with the blockchain and manufacturing communities) and will run the FMCG Open Innovation Community with the purpose to bring relevant stakeholders together and find research and business opportunities. Moreover, DCC will run workshops to identify new business models emerging for start-ups and collaborators and will produce reports from the results for knowledge sharing and to raise awareness of DIY4U.  
PIC  
PICOM (French innovation cluster working for the retail industry) will lead use case definition and business model definition as well as evaluation, ensuring that they address both customers, brands, retailers, service providers, and technology providers' needs. PICOM will also provide strong support to open calls management and dissemination.  
CDY  
Cody (robotic and automation solution provider with expertise in manufacturing automated equipment) will lead the Fablab design and development. Cody will contribute with their knowledge within machine development, product development, machine safety, operator interface, and interface between machines and platform. Cody will also lead the Fablab replication and DIY4U training.  
RDI  
RDI will analyze market and segment sectors related to DIY4U. We will ensure data repository and analytics in order to provide valuable information and non-trivial correlation between parameters, assess human behavior and needs. Also, RDI will create Machine Learning to evaluate created formulas and make decisions.  
STL  
Stelar (“Privacy-by-Design” experts with legal background) will contribute legal data privacy requirements analysis and GDPR/other regulations compliance support monitoring, ensuring that DIY4U complies with relevant regulations.  
EFF  
Effective Decisions, actively involved in the blockchain space in the last 4 years, will contribute with high-level architecture perspective and development expertise for the decentralized modules, distributed ledger technologies, and privacy-preserving features of the DIY4U platform.  
DSC  
DSC (soft matter modelling and process simulation experts) will lead the development of relevant soft matter models and support CTM with the development of the Fablab digital Twin, working in close collaboration with STEF, CPI, and the rest of our consortium.

1. Excellence  
1.0 Introduction to the industrial need  
Lightweight materials have become increasingly critical for producing components for aircrafts, cars, trains, ships, and defence equipment. Especially, lightweight metal and alloys possess high strength-to-weight ratios and low density. [1] Common applications are met in the automotive, aerospace, manufacturing, railway and other large niche applications. For example, lighter vehicles consume less fuel, emit less harmful gases and provide a better performance. It should be noted that the aforementioned sectors (based on recent Eurostat data [2]) are of crucial importance for the European Manufacturing Industry providing more than 6 million jobs and contributing ~11% to the EC-28 GDP. As lightweight metals we consider aluminum, magnesium, and titanium alloys. Moreover, metal matrix composite materials that traditionally incorporate micron scale reinforcements in a bulk metallic matrix offer opportunities to tailor material properties such as hardness, tensile strength, thermal stability, ductility, density, thermal and electrical conductivity, and wear resistance. With the advent of nanomaterials, nanocomposites are envisioned, and are being developed, with properties that overcome technical limitations of pure metals or composites that contain micron scale reinforcements. In the past decade, much work has been done towards developing polymer matrix nanocomposites and such materials are already used in various applications. [3] However, metal matrix composites containing nanoparticles (NPs) or carbon nanotubes could offer distinct advantages over polymer composites due to the inherent high temperature stability, high strength, high stiffness, wear resistance, thermal and electrical conductivity of the metal matrix. [4] Our project aspires to be a point of reference for boosting innovation in the field of lightweight metal matrix nanocomposites (MMnC) setting up an Open Innovation Ecosystem (test bed) that will boost the introduction of new functionalities, features and capabilities to lightweight metals. The LightMe Ecosystem will provide the necessary infrastructure (6 Pilot Lines – PL) and knowhow for upscaling the new materials concepts related to lightweight MMnC and advance materials, in a cost effective and sustainable way. Besides the upscaling units, the Ecosystem will provide services including monitoring, testing, modelling & simulation, standardization, regulatory compliance, nanosafety and innovation management (IM) addressing the industrial needs of SMEs and large enterprises that will access the Ecosystem and thus, ensuring the technology transfer to the market. (Figure 1.1).  
The LightMe ecosystem  
Figure 1.1: LightMe open access innovation Ecosystem structure will consist of 17 core aligned and complementary partners, members of our consortium, established in 11 EC countries and will offer open, non-discriminated access to clients, including SMEs and start-ups, acting as a one stop shop. The LightMe ecosystem will be represented by a legal entity – a Non for Profit Organization (NPO) – that will be the Single Entry Point for the customers – and will have the mandate to sign contracts on behalf of the Ecosystem. The positioning of LightMe in the innovation value chain will allow us to function as a bridge between upstream (material developers and knowhow suppliers as Universities and Research Centers) and downstream (end users) industries.  
1.1 Objectives  
A. Objectives related to the set up and operation of the LightMe Ecosystem  
1. Set up a sustainable open innovation ecosystem for efficient, low cost and fast upscaling of lightweight metal alloys and nanocomposites and related products, consisting of 6 operational building blocks (Figure 1.1)  
2. Determine administrative structure and define procedures and roles – avoiding any overlapping that can potentially lead to internal conflicts – securing a smooth operation of the ecosystem,  
3. Establish a Non-for-profit Organization acting as a Single-Entry Point (SEP) for the clients of the ecosystem and undertaking the responsibility of administrative and legal aspects.  
4. Clarify governance, organization and operational procedures, including knowledge management and IPR handling between the members of the Ecosystem as well as between the clients and the ecosystem  
5. Develop a decision-making process in the ecosystem enabling the identification and evaluation of the needs of test cases brought by clients and translating them in upscaling, characterization and services workflows.  
6. Enhance the operation and implement additional features to the 6 PL of the ecosystem in order to cope with the industrial requirements demanded by the customers (SMEs, industries) in terms of novel lightweight products development including usage of nanoparticles.  
7. To integrate novel introduction method by ultrasound of nanoparticles in a batch process with suitable volume. For LPDC in a pilot scale production up to 150 kg and in rapid processing of small volumes for GSC.  
8. To increase volume production of HPDC by optimizing the following processing parameters: melt volume, melt temperature, stirring rate (mechanical stirring), stirring vane design, ultrasonic probe shape/material and depth, ultrasonic frequencies and pulse rates, and melt feed rates into the shot die. Modelling can be utilized to determine the effect of NPs on the die filling of the composite and thus limit defects. By determining the optimum NP addition levels, pouring temperatures, and plunger speeds scrap levels can be reduced thereby increasing production rates. The modelling will give insights into optimal casting for changing component geometries and alloys  
9. For the WLAM pilot line, a specific procedure for process parameters development will be employed consisting of 3 steps: 1) definition of the AM setup, 2) DoE for process window adjustment and 3) validation through manufacturing of standard test artefacts. All test results (imaging and material characterization), stored continuously in a DB, will speed up the development of new MMnC by WLAM based on previous developments stored in the DB making each new development faster.  
10. Reduce by 70% the amount of faulty parts per day produced by the Borealis machine thanks to the integration of a closed loop control able to detect and correct online the deposition errors occurring during the DED process.  
11. To enhance the operation of SPS -KOBO PL modification of SPS tool and tool assembling system, improvement of KOBO process and tool design to increase a PL productivity (up to 20-40%) and decrease the production cost (up to 30%). SPS -KOBO pilot line targets in the production of lightweight bearing rings and profiles for cold forging of fasteners and other elements for aircraft/automotive industry from nanocomposite powders  
B. Objectives related to the validation and demonstration of the LightMe ecosystem  
12. Validate the proper operation of the ecosystem based on 8 selected test cases for upscaling, testing and demonstrating in operation environment of nanoenabled materials concept introduced by SMEs and industry demonstrating a 20% faster validation of materials compared to current SoA validation methodologies.  
13. Launch an open call of interest for test cases (at least 10) dedicated to SMEs that will be able to access the ecosystem during the last two years of our project for free.  
14. Proceed with improvements of operational procedures based on the feedback received from the test cases  
C. Objectives related to the LightMe sustainability  
15. To establish clustering and networking channels with EPPN (European Pilot Production Network), AM-Motion, European Materials Characterization and Modelling council etc., and other test beds (upscaling, characterization and modelling), particularly in the field of metal Alloys and composites, for cooperation and developing synergies towards a pan European integrated and sustainable ecosystem.  
16. Collaborate with European initiatives including open data repositories (e.g. EPPN, Nanosafety cluster, enanomapper etc) and regulatory agencies.  
17. Undertake communication/dissemination activities for promoting the ecosystem in targeted audiences, advertising the “success case” stories that will arise from the test cases.  
18. To continuously update the initial business plan of the LightMe ecosystem  
19. Liaise with potential investors (venture capitals and business angels) seeking extra funding and thus facilitating the access to funding for potential customers as well as with relevant Associations.  
1.2 Relation to the work programme  
Specific challenge  
The field of new smart lightweight nano-enabled materials … composite components and structures. We have sustained efforts since the 1970’s oil crises to reduce weight in transport systems in order to achieve improved energy efficiency. [6] Especially, when it comes to lightweight metals, Al, Mg and Ti alloys have been applied in various cases. Their wider applicability in the manufacturing industry demands enhancement of their mechanical properties as well as other functions. For example, although Mg is the lightest structural metal, its strength and stiffness need to be increased in order to be applied in high demand applications. Ceramic particles have long been considered as a potential way to make metals stronger. However, with microscale ceramic particles, the infusion process results in a loss of plasticity. [7] Nanoscale particles, in contrast, can enhance strength (due to phenomena like load transfer effect, Hall-Petch and Orowan Strengthening), maintain or even improve metals’ plasticity (CTE and EM mismatch effect) [8, 9], even provide additional functionalities e.g. self-cleaning, anti-bacterial as in the case of TiO2 NPs, or self-lubrication for the case of Graphene or MoS2. The greatest challenges we have faced for the development of MMnCs in wide application were on one hand the cost of nanoscale reinforcements and on the other hand the cost and the complexity in synthesis and processing of nanocomposites. [4] In the last years, advances in synthesis of NPs have led to the decrease of their cost while, new technologies (e.g. additive manufacturing – 3D printing) have allowed the design and manufacturing of geometrical complex objects. 1 Excellence  
We will bring together snapshot mosaic filters, OLED-based sensors, and state-of-the-art machine learning to deliver breakthrough and cost-effective snapshot hyperspectral imaging and spectrometric solutions covering a broad spectral range and suited to our actual industrial monitoring and control needs. Our multimodal monitoring systems will be IoT native, exploiting open source cloud, big data, and deep learning technology. A fast orchestrated deployment of data-driven AI-based models will foster production optimization. The affordability of monolithic uncooled CMOS and CCD sensors sensitive in the visible (VIS) and part of the near infrared (NIR) range has fuelled the spread of machine vision and photonic sensors throughout machinery and production lines. They are necessary elements of any automated production line we can imagine, monitoring a wide variety of magnitudes and providing feedback for optimizing manufacturing processes and product quality. Europe has played a clearly leading role in the field with key suppliers of sensors (e.g. AMS), cameras (e.g. Basler, JAI, PhotonFocus), software (e.g. MVTec) and a vibrant network of SMEs and midcaps delivering turnkey solutions for process monitoring and control to virtually any manufacturing sector.  
While hybrid and cooled sensor and FPA technologies (e.g. InGaAs, InSb, MCT) have been traditionally regarded as expensive and bulky, the high demand and technical advances on InGaAs FPAs have cut down their cost making them attractive for the industrial vision market. Relevant camera suppliers in this segment like PhotonFocus already market InGaAs cameras with tag prices under the 10,000€ barrier, with the expectation of further cuts in the short-medium term. Besides, new technologies like OLED sensors are set to make a breakthrough in terms of cost and performance and have already shown a big potential in chemometric applications.  
The last years have seen a research boom in hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and spectroscopy measurement technologies, especially in a spectral range that spans the visible, short-wavelength infrared (SWIR), and mid-wavelength infrared (MWIR). This part of the optical spectrum has proved as a privileged proxy to material properties, especially suited to non-destructive monitoring. It avoids the damage and risks of ionising radiation, and also the environmental noise of thermal radiation. Compared to widespread panchromatic or trichromatic systems, spectrally resolved technology has demonstrated far higher accuracy and specificity, key to address more and more scenarios of industrial monitoring and control, across many different sectors.  
This context explains the optimistic forecasts on the hyperspectral imaging systems market, projected to reach USD 21.33 bn by 2023 from USD 8.14 bn in 2017, at a strong CAGR of 17.5%1. Hyperspectral imaging -i.e. sensing with a crowd of finer spectral bands - has been associated to bulky and expensive spectrometers only capable of push-broom imaging, not suitable to take snapshots, while the term snapshot has been largely associated to a few spectral bands, with configurations other than Bayer-based or filter-based solutions both bulky and expensive.  
Mosaic pixel-level filters by IMEC have set a breakthrough in the field, enabling snapshot hyperspectral imaging in the visible range first, and in the SWIR range more recently. This technology -as a coating process - is suitable for volume production and does not alter the compactness of imagers. That is, it is suited to deliver low SWaPC (size, weight, power and cost) solutions.  
Moreover, the maturity of computational imaging for dual and multi-aperture optics -already empowering many smartphone cameras- brings a clear opportunity. Combined with the feasibility of cost-effective snapshot HSI camera cores in the SWIR and VIS range, it sets the conditions to deliver novel snapshot HSI VIS/SWIR imagers with killing price and capabilities. The promise of bringing to the market broadband highly selective hyperspectral imaging covering what is likely the most important spectral range for process and product monitoring is only a collaboration away. A collaboration of few European organisations.  
The use of low-cost high-performance OLED sensors, together with advanced laser-based concepts, in the context of chemometric analysis offers clear opportunities to deliver novel on-line and in situ analysers in an even broader spectral range with a big impact on process and product quality.  
The development of models for measurement and classification is still prone to handcrafted engineering workflows (costly and slow). Desktop software libraries and small sets of data are the main and most usual resources used for modelling, building, and testing. When faced with high dimensional data (e.g. hyperspectral imaging) this burden is likely to discourage our development effort. It is for a reason that most results reported on HSI have an academic source and few commercial solutions are available yet compared to the wide adoption of standard vision systems.  
The recent success of data-driven approaches and deep neural networks -especially for image analysis - enables a faster development of models with better regression, classification, or segmentation accuracy. While the scarcity of suitable data for training has remained a barrier for adoption, new cloud-based architectures have demonstrated the capability to collect, store, and annotate big data, enabling the training of new models in a fraction of time.

Moreover, the orchestration of large sets of data sensors in other sectors -like video surveillance for security or data analytics in omnichannel retail- are successfully exploiting cloud services and big data technology for data collection and analytics and large scale training and deployment of deep neural models.  
Current monitoring systems mostly work in isolation to monitor or control a given specific operation. The combination of last edge technology and cloud-services for the orchestration of sensors (e.g. HSI sensors), big data collection and analysis, and large-scale training and deployment of deep neural models on the edge, enables the integral management of data and their use for process optimization and analytics.  

Figure 1. Our concept.  
We will deliver breakthrough and cost-effective snapshot HSI cameras in the broad VIS/SWIR range, complemented with cost-effective OLED-based and laser-based chemometric analysers. On top of these sensors, we will develop novel multimodal monitoring systems that will be IoT native, by combining them with open source cloud, big data, and deep learning technology. An agile development and orchestration of complex AI-based models that optimize production will accelerate innovation schemes in relevant manufacturing scenarios.  
1.1 Objectives  
The main goal of our project is the development of cost-effective multimodal monitoring solutions with a breakthrough impact on production quality and efficiency. Key technology features pursued by us are a broader spectral range with snapshot hyperspectral capabilities, and an IoT native approach to the development and deployment of machine learning models (i.e. statistical and deep learning models) for overall monitoring and control of production lines.  
With this aim, we will pursue the following technical goals:  
1. The development of cost-effective HSI SWIR (0.9-1.7µm wavelength) camera cores ready for volume production, covering a missing offer highly demanded by the market.  
2. The development of dual aperture snapshot HSI imagers combining snapshot CMOS and InGaAs hyperspectral sensors based on mosaic filters, delivering breakthrough snapshot hyperspectral imaging (HSI) capabilities in the whole VIS/SWIR range (0.4-1.7µm wavelength).  
3. The development of novel spectrometric solutions for accurate highly specific chemometric analysis in an extended VIS/MWIR (0.4-5µm wavelength) range. Innovative concepts in OLED sensors, laser-based spectroscopy, and signal processing will be combined in our project to deliver new spectrometric solutions with breakthrough sensitivity and performance.

Excellence 1.1 Objectives Introduction The occurrence of fungal and subsequently, mycotoxin contamination in various crops is of major concern since it has significant implications for food and feed safety, food security and international trade. We produce yearly about 133 million tons (MT) wheat (~M€ 29038), 68 MT maize (~M€ 13571) and 8 MT oats (~M€ 1543)1,2. Results using latest state-of-the-art multi-analyte methods show that almost 100% of our crops are contaminated with one or more mycotoxins3. With annual losses due to mycotoxins estimated at 5 -10%, this equates to €1.2 -2.4 billion (Bn) in lost income for wheat alone, a reduction in these losses of only 1% would save €12 -24 Mio. Moreover with ‘’one in eight persons worldwide suffering from chronic undernourishment’’ 4; there is clearly a moral obligation for us to curb these significant food and feed losses. Grain and foods based on these grains (e.g. pasta, bread, bakery products) account for the largest contribution to mycotoxin exposure in all age classes of the EU population, in particular due to the mycotoxins produced by Fusarium spp. (deoxynivalenol [DON], T-2/HT-2 toxins, zearalenone [ZEN], ochratoxin A [OTA] and fumonisins). Despite huge research investments, prevention and control of these toxic secondary metabolites remain difficult and we continue to be vulnerable to problems of contamination: In February–March 2013 Romania, Serbia and Croatia reported aflatoxin M1 contamination of milk. Severe droughts in Serbia in 2012 resulted in 70% of the maize crop being contaminated with aflatoxins 5. Use of this maize to feed dairy cattle led to the high levels of aflatoxin M1 in milk, up to twice the EU legal limit. The milk scandal was fuelled when the permitted level of aflatoxin M1 in milk in Serbia was temporarily raised to 0.5 µg/kg milk, 10 times the EU legal limit. In recent years, a dramatic increase in DON contamination in Nordic countries has been observed. The Norwegian Scientific Committee for Food Safety concluded in a study published in 2013 that “The estimated mean and high (95-percentile) exposures to DON ...were in the range of, or exceeded the TDI by almost 2 times in 1-year-old infants and 2-year-old children and up to 3.5 times in years with high DON levels which has been especially true for oat-based products 6. On the other hand, the catastrophic floods and the rainy summer in 2014 resulted in low levels of aflatoxin B1 with high levels of DON. Obviously, extreme weather conditions as a result of climate change are increasingly affecting the mycotoxin map in Europe and also worldwide. In 2013 our EU’s RASFF7 (Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed) showed that of the total rejections at the EU borders, 18.7% were due to mycotoxin contamination exceeding the EU legislative limits8. The RASFF notification system also reveals that among the susceptible fruit imported into the EU, the commodity with the highest risk for aflatoxin contamination was dried figs. In addition, over the last five years RASFF reported 292 alerts relating to aflatoxin levels exceeding legal limits in peanuts imported from China. Exposure of livestock to mycotoxins may lead to substantial economic losses (Wu & Munkvold 2008) 9. While mycotoxicoses in agricultural animals are regularly reported (Jovanović et al. 2015)10, it is difficult for us to get an accurate estimate of the burden of market loss associated with animal health and productivity, and loss of animal feed. However, several publications from different parts of the world provide some insight into the potential magnitude of the problem. In the USA, Vardon et al. (2003)11 estimated a loss of 225 million US$ per annum for feed maize due to contamination with aflatoxins, and an additional loss of livestock of 4 million US$ per annum. Wu and Munkvold (2008) estimated the losses due to decreased weight gain in pigs resulting from fumonisin contamination of DDGS in the USA at 147 million US$ annually (20% DDGS in the diets and market penetration is 100%). Excellence 1.1 Objectives The main objective of our project is to develop strategies for rice productivity, stability, and quality by developing new commercial European rice (Oryza sativa) harboring salt tolerance alleles to protect the rice sector against climate change and apple snail (Pomacea insularum) invasion (NEURICE). European rice consumption was ca. 2.68 million tonnes of milled rice (Mt) in 2014, while our rice production in Europe (EU-28) for the same year was ca. 1.64 Mt [1], being mainly temperate japonica rice. Our rice production from 2013 to 2014 decreased from 1.77 Mt to 1.64 Mt; consequently, our rice imports have increased from 0.9 Mt in 2013 to 1.1 Mt in 2014, being mainly long-grain indica rice. Our rice exports (mainly japonica rice) were maintained at 0.2 Mt [1]. Thus, only 61% of the rice consumed by European citizens in 2014 was produced in the EU [1]. European rice productivity is affected by abiotic and biotic stresses and by local farming practices. Current European rice genotypes are not able to withstand salinity increases that the environment is posing on the rice-producing regions due to climate change; moreover, poor management has been employed to afford this in a proper way. Of particular concern, global temperature has increased over the last century, especially during the last 50 years (0.13° C / decade). This warming has been more intense in the Mediterranean region [2] and has been especially evident in spring and summer. Temperature increases around the Mediterranean coasts are greater than those observed in other regions of the same latitude. In addition, due to scarcer water availability and the rise in sea levels, there is a clear tendency toward salinization in the river deltas where we grow rice in Europe, notably in the plots close to sea level [2]. Salinization certainly affects rice as it is the most salt-sensitive crop among cereals [3, 4]. Associated with changes in temperature, the biotic stress of the apple snail species from genus Pomacea now threatens to destroy our rice paddy fields by eating the sown seed and the rice plantlets, representing one of the worst introduced gastropod crop pests of recent times [5-10]. It is calculated that nowadays this pest causes damages in rice fields worldwide that result in losses of tens of billions of Euros a year [11]. Recently, apple snails have been detected in the Ebro river delta, and now it represents an important threat to Europe’s wetlands biodiversity and rice production. To date, the measures we have adopted to combat the apple snail have failed, but in the autumn of 2013, a new strategy with permission from the EU was explored, which consisted of flooding 2500 ha of infested fields with seawater to eradicate the apple snails. This treatment proved 100% effective in destroying apple snail infestations [12]; nevertheless, residual salt concentrations affected negatively our rice productivity. There is a way to bypass this problem, which is to introgress salt tolerance into elite European varieties using the most salt-resistant alleles from tolerant rice lines such as Pokkali, FL478, and IR64-Saltol. It has been reported that Pokkali and FL478 can grow at 150 mM NaCl concentrations [13], while the most salt-tolerant European cultivars can only tolerate salinity ranges between 30 and 60 mM. The European deltaic areas have been historically and naturally salinized before rice cultivation, when artificial inundation during the dry season (summer) reduced salinity levels to permit rice cultivation; thus, the natural flora and fauna are not negatively affected by the seawater treatment. Our NEURICE project proposes a coordinated multi-actor approach that includes public research and technological development universities and the active participation of end-users such as technological companies, a specialized technological center for breeding and innovation support, and farmers' cooperatives. Our NEURICE project aims to address rice abiotic (i.e., salinity in fields caused by the rise in seawater level and scarcity of water availability due to climatic change effects) and biotic (i.e., apple snail pest) stresses. We ensure the generation and growth of ideas between all partners to develop the co-creation and co-ownership of new knowledge, technologies, and techniques that will facilitate their adoption for the benefit of the rice production sector as well as the generation of more jobs in Europe. NEURICE 4 Thus, the general objective of our NEURICE project is to identify and introduce genetic variation in European rice varieties for obtaining commercial varieties tolerant to salinity in order to: (i) mitigate the imminent effects of salinization and deterioration of water quality in the Mediterranean basins due to climate change; and (ii) avoid the decline in production observed after seawater treatments performed in rice paddies that successfully controlled the apple snail pest (which has recently appeared in a major producing region of Europe, the Ebro delta) but is likely to spread to other rice-growing areas in Europe, producing great losses [14]. For the seed industry partners from participating countries, the final objective of our NEURICE project is to register and commercialize new salt-tolerant elite European lines. These new salt-tolerant European commercial lines will become available all over Europe to use them as prevention against abiotic stress derived from climate change as well as for avoiding the dispersion of this devastating pest (biotic stress) apple snail all over Europe while maintaining or increasing rice production. These new rice lines will allow rice cultivation in abandoned salinized paddies, while low productive salinized fields will obtain higher yields resulting in the creation of new jobs and a reduction of rice imports in Europe. The specific objectives of our NEURICE project are: 1. To identify new salt-tolerant varieties and new salt tolerance alleles using phenotypical, physiological, and genetic screening of germplasm collection and complementary approaches of Genome Wide Association Study (GWAS) in a panel of japonica accessions and linkage mapping in a population of biparental Recombinant Inbred Lines (RILs) (UMIL, CRA, UGLA, INDEAR, and CAAS). We have selected partners for this proposal that include European teams with outstanding expertise in rice genotyping/phenotyping and top worldwide teams in biotechnology from third-country partners (i.e., Argentina and People’s Republic of China). By the 24th month of our NEURICE project, we will identify salt-tolerant japonica accessions and biparental RILs; moreover, at month 48, the genomic studies will be complete. 2. To obtain advanced lines with improved salt tolerance by introgressing Saltol, a chromosome segment carrying a salt tolerance gene from the highly salt-tolerant Asian variety FL478 into our European elite varieties from Spain, Italy, and France using marker-assisted backcross (MABC) selection (UB, UMIL, CRAG, CIRAD, CRA, UGLA, and CFR). This objective also includes an embryo rescue adaptation to fasten the (MABC) process. The best rice centers from the main rice production areas have been carefully selected as partners. At the 22nd month of our project, BC3F3 seeds (the 3rd generation of the 3rd backcross) will be ready to be assayed in controlled conditions using hydroponic systems to validate their salinity tolerance. 3. To evaluate salinity tolerance in controlled conditions using hydroponic systems to validate salt-tolerant lines produced within our project after introgressing salt tolerance genes from FL478 into our European elite varieties (UB, CRAG, CIRAD, UMIL, and UGLA), including the most salt-tolerant japonica accessions and RILs. This evaluation will be complemented with the quantification of salinity-related transport and accumulation traits (UGLA) and oxidative stress markers (UB). The seeds from the validated lines will be obtained in month 27 to be used in field trials under non-salinized and salinized conditions. 4. To perform field trials to evaluate in salinized and non-salinized conditions the traditional European rice lines harboring the Saltol salt tolerance chromosomal region introgressed from salt-tolerant FL478, as well as the most salt-tolerant japonica accessions and RILs, in order to select those salt-tolerant lines with commercial potential. Each country will assess their lines in their low salinity environmental conditions to ensure quality, productivity, and commerciality of lines (CAMARA in Spain, CRA in Italy, and CFR in France). In parallel, the same lines will be assessed in high salinity conditions field trials (IRTA in Spain, SIS in Italy, and CFR in France). Samples from lines assessed in both salinity levels will be studied in order to select those salt-tolerant lines with the best commercial potentiality with the objective of registering the best for commercialization. 3.3 Consortium as a whole  
Our PHOTONFOOD consortium has a wide geographical and cultural coverage across Europe (Fig. 18). This naturally provides us with an understanding of different business and healthcare practices, while enabling us to access several stakeholders and end-users, providing wide feedback and promoting our project.  
We, the Norwegian University of Life Sciences (NMBU), are located close to Oslo and will contribute top expertise in spectroscopy, data analysis, and experience in food science, microbiology, user acceptance, and innovation. We will provide the scientific manager, coordinate our project, lead WP8, and provide project manager and coordinator. We have recently started Norway’s first data science program and have top expertise in data analysis related to spectroscopy. We will lead the data analysis and software development in WP5. We also have top expertise in innovation and will provide the innovation manager for our project.  
We, the University of Ulm (UULM), located in the south of Germany, represented by the Institute of Analytical and Bio-analytical Chemistry, are worldwide leaders in the development and application of innovative (micro- and nano-) analytical platforms, providing advanced methods and technologies in sensing, diagnostics, and imaging of complex molecular processes relevant to environmental, industrial, and medical/biological applications. We will lead WP3 and be responsible for developing the prototypes of the HI-FI and HI-FI devices.  
We, the University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences, Vienna (BOKU), located in Austria and represented by the Department of Agrobiotechnology (IFA-Tulln), are a leading institution in the area of agro-biotechnology with a special emphasis on mycotoxin control and reduction since 1994. We will lead WP6 on Demonstration and validation. We will also summarize the feedback of WP1 on the usability of the PHOTONFOOD.

Description of our legal entity  
We, the Norwegian University of Life Sciences (NMBU) in Ås, are Norway’s largest life sciences university with 5100 students; 1700 employees; 800 scientific staff; 580 PhD students; approx. 100 PhD degrees per year.  
• The Faculty of Science of Technology (REALTEK) at NMBU has key expertise in biophysics, spectroscopy, and data analysis. We have invested heavily in being at the forefront of advanced multivariate data analysis and vibrational spectroscopic techniques (NIR, FTIR, and Raman, etc.). Today, “Biospectroscopy and data modelling” (BioSpec) is identified as one of the main research areas at REALTEK. Our BioSpec group is at the forefront of the application of vibrational spectroscopy techniques in life sciences and data science for vibrational spectroscopy. The group consists on average of 20 PhDs, PostDocs, and Researchers. The academic staff of our group consists of Prof. Dr. Achim Kohler and Assoc. Prof. Dr. Volha Shapaval. We have pioneered the development of algorithms for scatter correction and signal standardization in infrared spectroscopy. Over many years, we have been developing so-called model-based pre-treatment approaches for spectral pre-treatment and data analysis methods for calibration and classification, which are in use by a broad community in infrared spectroscopy in life sciences and biomedical vibrational spectroscopy.  
• From the School of Economics and Business at NMBU, our Analytics, Innovation, and Digital Business Transformation group will participate in our project. The group is led by Prof. Joachim Scholderer and consists of two professors, two associate professors, one postdoctoral fellow, and four PhD students. We cooperate closely with NMBU’s TTO and the various incubators and accelerators in NMBU’s innovation ecosystem. We will contribute our expertise in the management of life science technology innovations, including IP strategy, market potential assessment, stakeholder and user acceptance along the value chain, forecasting, risk assessment, and commercialization.  
Main role and task in our project  
Our BioSpec group, headed by Prof. Achim Kohler (M), will coordinate our project and lead the research related to WP5. We will be strongly involved in spectroscopic measurements with benchmark and PHOTONFOOD devices in WP6. The group of Prof. Joachim Scholderer at the NMBU School of Economics and Business will be involved in the usability and technical-economic assessment activities (WP1), the demonstrations (WP6), the exploitation of the results (WP7), and the project management (WP8). Prof. Scholderer will be the Innovation Manager of our project.

Excellence 1.1 Objectives While Europe depends heavily on fossil-derived plastics and consumes in the range of 50 Million tonnes per year, only 32% of the resulting waste is currently recycled1 (with a negligible part going to organic recycling) and a larger share is incinerated or landfilled. This contributes to the depletion of our limited resources and is hard to accept in view of our EU’s resource efficiency objectives. Besides, according to the Ellen MacArthur Foundation2, as much as 32% of the plastic packaging ends up leaking in the environment, outside of controlled end of life (EoL) management systems leading to dramatic long-term pollution. Given that packaging consumes more than 40% of all plastic produced each year, improving its circularity from origin to subsequent life cycles is more than urgent. Furthermore, despite our growing market (CAGR 20%3), bioplastics still have a very limited share among plastics (approximately 1-2%). They can, however, provide advantages in terms of switching to renewable feedstocks and EoL modularity. Nevertheless, while some drop-in solutions exist, there are still unmet demands for which specialty bio-based materials need to be developed. Furthermore, the biodegradability of available biodegradable biopolymers is not possible in every environment and their recycling is also insufficiently established. Building upon a number of past projects and in synergies with ongoing projects such as Refucoat, Decoat, Terminus, ECOat, BIOnTop, etc. (Table 1.3), we will enhance bio-based packaging in terms of properties that currently limit the application of bioplastics as well as in terms of EoL. Our upcycling strategies to boost packaging performance in the first and subsequent life cycles of the materials comprise: 1. Application of previously developed biobased coatings and adhesives that will be tailored and used in new materials combinations that are easy to sort and recycle: • Removable optionally metallised oxygen barrier protein coatings and hydrophobic fatty acid grafting technologies that decrease biopolymer gas permeability to permit packing sensitive food products which, in spite of being applied in multilayer films, do not jeopardize their posterior recyclability or biodegradability. • Removable primers/adhesives for multilayer films involving adhesive lamination as well as for allowing the separation of labels from the packaging. • PHA coatings providing barrier to water vapour, liquid food/beverages and grease, especially for paper/cardboard/pulp-based beverage packaging. 2. Application of eBeam radiation that has shown, at lab scale, the ability to increase the barrier and mechanical properties of biopolymers and increase the compatibility of polymer blends. Despite the fact that this technique shows good industrial potential as an emerging decontamination step in packaging lines, it has not been applied in the intended scope at a higher TRL. 3. To compensate secondary raw materials (SRM) downgrading during material reprocessing, we will deliver recycled bioplastics with improved properties through self-reinforcement. While this can also be applied on virgin materials, it is especially innovative for reprocessing bicomponent packaging (e.g. coextruded films) without needing to separate the layers. 4. Furthermore, more conventional addition such as chain extenders as well as compatibilizers (in the case of blends) will be pursued. 1 Plastics – the Facts 2019 An analysis of European plastics production, demand and waste data 2 THE NEW PLASTICS ECONOMY RETHINKING THE FUTURE OF PLASTICS, Ellen MacArthur foundation, 2016 3 Source partner EUBP (European Bioplastics association) Figure 1.1 Life cycle of PRESERVE upcycled innovative materials (minor unavoidable waste due to non-mature collection system or littering not represented) This proposal version was submitted by Elodie BUGNICOURT on 14/05/2020 16:20:41 Brussels Local Time. Embedding enzymes in the bioplastics (enzymation) based on technologies patented by Carbios. This will allow PLA to be enhanced to biodegrade even at greater thickness or in milder conditions than standard PLA thanks to the previously discussed strategies. 2. Enzymatic controlled recycling will be applied to polyesters, such as PLA, and coated/multilayer PLA materials in order to obtain oligomers and preserve the majority of covalent bonds present along the macromolecular chains to retain the energy spent to create them. The oligomers will be exploited as additives and building blocks to synthesize new polymers with chemical structure specifically designed for upcycled secondary applications in coating and adhesive fields. 3. Adhesive or coating removal using enzymatic detergents during cleaning of EoL packaging for delamination. This will be an optional process before reprocessing and upcycling of mono and multilayer bioplastic food and drink packaging or before repulping of the fibre-based packaging. The food and drink packaging sector is the main target of our PRESERVE primary materials. But in keeping with current legislations, to create a market for our SRMs and although their applicability for food packaging will be checked with migration tests, these recovered polymers will be initially mainly targeted at non-food-contact applications such as packaging for personal care products as well as reusable textiles and composites secondary packaging, equally relevant to transport food and drinks, as upcycling demonstrators4. The versatile compatibility of our solutions with different existing and enhanced EoL options is key to adjusting to the wide range of packed products and formats (e.g. in case of significant product leftover) as well as to the highly variable waste management infrastructures across Europe to eventually lead to significantly reducing landfill, incineration and pollution. The impact of enzymation as well as functional finishing treatments on the prevention of microplastics release in the environment due to unfortunate incidents littering will also be assessed. For the different stakeholders to define the best upcycling approach based on a case-by-case multiparametric optimisation, a decision support system (DSS) will be designed. We combine pre-existing advances with breakthrough circular approaches and address the challenges towards their market uptake. We will therefore demonstrate that our solutions match the requirements for the most widely used packaging materials and formats from large converters such as SUDPACK, GPI and SIBO and end users like MDLZ, BDF, DANONE (and CocaCola5 among others) who have been pioneers in the market introduction of bio-packaging. We have selected bioplastics that are readily integrated in existing packaging production lines (“drop in” and/or compatible with standard processes) and thanks to an ambitious exploitation roadmap, we expect the PRESERVE innovations to show the potential to substitute more than 60% of all food and drinks packaging by 2030 (details in Table 1.4). The added value of the secondary applications thanks to the upcycled bioplastics performance will greatly incentivize the adoption of such new solutions. This will also be supported by outreach activities towards the related stakeholders and policy makers as well as social and consumers perception studies and awareness raising campaigns. A new value chain from biopolymer suppliers, packaging converters, end users, waste management companies, and technological support will be created. Recommendations for new incentives and standards tailored to bioplastics will be defined to support the commercial roll out of our solutions. Our PRESERVE circular renewably sourced packaging solutions and derived upcycled applications will optimally preserve the actual food and drinks, our finite material and energy resources and the environment.
